sections.heading: Modern use rose only slightly, but the method mix moved toward shorter-acting methods
sections.body: Modern contraceptive use among all women was 25.7 percent in 2025, up from 24.3 percent in 2022. The pill remained the largest modern method at 11.6 percent, while injectables rose from 2.8 to 3.4 percent and implants from 1.7 to 2.4 percent. Female sterilization edged down from 5.0 to 4.6 percent, so the modest national increase came more from reversible methods than from permanent methods.

sections.heading: The highest-use regions form a southern and island-heavy cluster
sections.body: Davao Region had the highest modern contraceptive use among all women at 37.1 percent, followed by MIMAROPA at 33.9 percent, SOCCSKSARGEN at 32.5 percent, and Caraga at 32.2 percent. These regions were not just slightly above the national level of 25.7 percent; each was at least 6.5 points higher. Their method mixes also leaned strongly on pills, with Davao at 18.6 percent, SOCCSKSARGEN at 17.1 percent, Caraga at 15.0 percent, and MIMAROPA at 14.4 percent.

sections.heading: Low modern use is concentrated in several very different regions
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest modern contraceptive use at 13.0 percent, but low levels were not limited to one setting. Central Visayas was also low at 21.0 percent, NCR at 21.4 percent, and Bicol Region at 22.1 percent. This matters because the lower end includes both a highly urban region and regions outside the metropolitan core, suggesting that regional differences are not just an urban-rural story.

sections.heading: Some regions changed direction while the national rate barely moved
sections.body: The national increase from 2022 to 2025 was only 1.4 points, but several regions moved much more. MIMAROPA rose from 26.8 to 33.9 percent, Davao Region from 32.3 to 37.1 percent, Eastern Visayas from 23.1 to 27.6 percent, and Central Luzon from 24.6 to 28.9 percent. By contrast, Central Visayas fell from 26.9 to 21.0 percent, Cagayan Valley from 34.3 to 28.7 percent, and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ from 28.5 to 24.7 percent.

sections.heading: Regional method mixes point to different access patterns
sections.body: The pill was the main modern method in most regions, but not every high or mid-level region arrived there the same way. MIMAROPA combined 14.4 percent pill use with the highest injectable level at 8.0 percent and the highest implant level at 5.8 percent. CAR stood out for female sterilization at 10.0 percent, while Northern Mindanao had the highest IUD level at 4.0 percent, showing that similar modern-use totals can hide very different method profiles.

sections.heading: Modern use peaks in the middle reproductive ages
sections.body: Modern contraceptive use was highest among women aged 35 to 39 at 41.1 percent, followed closely by ages 40 to 44 at 38.4 percent and 30 to 34 at 38.0 percent. Use was much lower among women aged 15 to 19 at 2.5 percent and 20 to 24 at 15.4 percent, reflecting the all-women denominator as well as different life stages. The method mix also changes with age: pill use peaks around ages 30 to 39, while female sterilization becomes much more visible from age 35 onward.

sections.heading: Modern use is highest among women with three or four children
sections.body: Women with three or four living children had the highest modern contraceptive use at 55.8 percent, compared with 46.5 percent among women with five or more children and 37.9 percent among women with one or two children. The same group also had the highest pill use at 22.6 percent and female sterilization at 15.9 percent. Women with no living children were far lower at 2.4 percent, which is expected in an all-women measure but still shows how strongly modern use is tied to family-building stage.

sections.heading: Rural women reported higher modern use than urban women
sections.body: Modern contraceptive use was 28.8 percent among rural women and 23.3 percent among urban women in 2025. The rural advantage was concentrated in the pill, at 14.2 percent compared with 9.6 percent in urban areas, and in implants, at 2.9 percent compared with 2.0 percent. Male condom use moved in the opposite direction, at 2.3 percent in urban areas and 1.3 percent in rural areas.

sections.heading: Education differences are uneven rather than linear
sections.body: Modern use was highest among women with Grades 1-6 education at 40.1 percent and lowest among women with Grades 11-12 at 11.8 percent. College-educated women were at 21.7 percent, while women with post-secondary education were at 32.0 percent. The pattern is not a simple education ladder, and the education table alone does not explain why these differences appear.

sections.heading: Modern use declines across wealth groups
sections.body: Modern contraceptive use was highest in the lowest wealth quintile at 32.5 percent and lowest in the highest quintile at 20.0 percent. The pill, injectables, and implants all followed this lower-to-higher wealth gradient, while male condom use was highest in the highest quintile at 3.1 percent. This means the wealth pattern is not only about total modern use; it is also about which modern methods are most visible in each wealth group.

takeaways: Modern contraceptive use among all women rose modestly to 25.7 percent in 2025, with pills still accounting for the largest share of modern use.
Regional differences are wide: Davao Region, MIMAROPA, SOCCSKSARGEN, and Caraga were far above the national level, while BARMM remained much lower.
The strongest subgroup differences appear by number of living children, wealth, education, and region, while the urban-rural gap is smaller but still favors rural women.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
